results.condition_check: The regulation allows an exception when processing without documented instructions is required by Union or Member State law. The contract does not mention this legal-requirement exception, so it is narrower and does not cover the same condition.
results.modality_check: The contract uses 'may only', which is restrictive and at least as strong as a 'shall' obligation, but it is missing the legal exception.
results.other_constraints_check: No conflicting timing/manner/location constraints beyond the missing lawful-processing exception; however the contract is genuinely looser because it omits the law-required carve-out.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor may only act and process the Personal Data in accordance with the documented instruction from the Data Controller (the ”Instruction”). The Instruction at the time of entering into this Data Processor Agreement is that the Data Processor may only process the Personal Data with the purpose of delivering the Main Services as described in the Main Agreement.

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the contract text is only a heading/topic label ('Data protection impact assessments and prior consultation') and does not itself impose any processor assistance obligation or state the required content.
results.condition_check: No condition stated in the contract text.
results.modality_check: No operative modality stated; only a heading.
results.other_constraints_check: No timing/manner/target/location constraints stated.
results.contract_source_text: Data protection impact assessments and prior consultation

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires the processor to assist the controller in communicating a personal data breach to the data subject, while the contract sub-item only requires a description of breach-response measures taken or proposed and mitigation effects; that is record/content about measures, not assistance with communicating to the data subject.
results.condition_check: Compatible only in the sense that both concern a personal data breach, but the triggering situation is different: breach-response documentation versus assistance in communication to data subjects.
results.modality_check: The contract sub-item is descriptive content within a mandatory register clause, but it does not impose the required assistance obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: No relevant timing/manner/location equivalence bridges the gap; the contract omits the communication-to-data-subjects assistance duty entirely.
results.contract_source_text: A description of the measures that the Data Processor has taken or proposes to take to address the Personal Data Breach, including, where appropriate, measures taken to mitigate its adverse effects.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires the processor to process personal data on documented instructions from the controller. The contract says the processor's processing of the controller's personal data in the three months after termination shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction, which directly addresses processing under controller instruction.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The contract limits this to the three months after termination and to the extent necessary and required under applicable law, but still ties the processing to the Instruction; these added limits do not make it weaker on the documented-instructions requirement.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses mandatory language for the processing clause and expressly deems that processing to be in accordance with the Instruction, which is at least as strong as the regulatory requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The object and target match: processing of the controller's personal data. The timing limitation after termination is an added constraint, not a contradiction, and the instruction reference is substantively equivalent to documented instructions.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. In the same period, the Data Processor is entitled to include the Personal Data in the Data Processor’s backup. The Data Processor’s processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data in the three months after the termination of this Data Processor Agreement shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction.

shared_groups: Data Return Deletion And Post-Termination Handling
results.core_alignment_check: Same actor (processor) and same subject matter (post-termination handling of personal data), but the contract only permits/continues processing for up to three months and backup inclusion; it does not require returning or deleting all personal data to the controller after service end.
results.condition_check: Regulation triggers after the end of the provision of services relating to processing; contract triggers after termination of the agreement and allows processing during the following three months, which is not the same obligation and is temporally looser.
results.modality_check: Regulation imposes a mandatory return-or-delete duty; contract uses permission/entitlement to continue processing and backup inclusion, not a mandatory return/delete obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation requires return or deletion of all personal data to the controller; the contract instead allows continued processing and backup retention for up to three months, which is contradictory/looser rather than equivalent.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. In the same period, the Data Processor is entitled to include the Personal Data in the Data Processor’s backup. The Data Processor’s processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data in the three months after the termination of this Data Processor Agreement shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction.

shared_groups: Data Return Deletion And Post-Termination Handling
results.core_alignment_check: Processor obligation after end of services: regulation requires return or deletion of all personal data to the controller; contract instead allows continued processing for up to three months after termination and backup inclusion, with no return/deletion duty stated.
results.condition_check: Regulation triggers after the end of the provision of services relating to processing; contract triggers after termination and permits continued processing during the following three months, which is not the same return/delete condition and does not add the required post-service disposal obligation.
results.modality_check: Regulation imposes a mandatory shall return or delete; contract uses permission/entitlement to continue processing and backup, not an equal-or-greater obligation to return or delete.
results.other_constraints_check: Timing is looser and contradictory: instead of immediate post-service return/deletion, the contract authorizes retention/processing for up to three months after termination and treats it as instructed processing.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. In the same period, the Data Processor is entitled to include the Personal Data in the Data Processor’s backup. The Data Processor’s processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data in the three months after the termination of this Data Processor Agreement shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires the processor to process personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, while the contract sub-item merely says the processor complies with its obligations under the agreement and the Instruction; it does not itself state the processing-on-instructions obligation.
results.condition_check: No specific triggering condition in the regulation beyond documented instructions; the contract clause does not address that condition.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a compliance statement, but not an explicit obligation to process personal data on documented instructions.
results.other_constraints_check: No equivalent timing/manner/location constraints for the required processing-on-instructions duty are stated here.
results.contract_source_text: the Data Processor complies with its obligations under this Data Processor Agreement and the Instruction; and

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation imposes controller liability for damage caused by infringing processing; the contract instead requires the controller to indemnify the processor for documented additional costs caused by changes in law/guidance/court practice. Different actor, object, recipient, and trigger.
results.condition_check: Different condition: regulatory trigger is damage caused by GDPR-infringing processing; contract trigger is changes in applicable law/guidance/court practice causing extra processor costs.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a mandatory indemnity, but it is for a different obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: Different subject matter and remedy: indemnification of costs versus liability for damage.
results.contract_source_text: If changes to the Applicable Law, including new guidance or courts practice, result in additional costs to the Data Processor, the Data Controller shall indemnify the Data Processor of such documented costs. 8. Breach and liability

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires a specific processor duty to ensure persons acting under its authority with access to personal data process only on controller instructions; the contract clause only gives a general promise to comply with applicable law regarding processing of the controller's personal data, which does not specifically address authorized persons, access, or instruction-only processing.
results.condition_check: No specific triggering condition in the contract matches the regulation's condition about persons acting under the processor's authority with access to personal data.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a general compliance obligation, but it does not impose the same substantive duty; it is not a direct equivalent of the required instruction-only restriction.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation's target is a restriction on how authorized persons process data; the contract instead concerns general lawful processing and, in the parent context, documentation after written request, which is a different duty.
results.contract_source_text: the Data Processor complies with the Applicable Law in respect of the processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation requires prior specific or general written authorization from the controller before engaging a sub-processor, while the contract requires a written sub-processor agreement and monitoring/control of sub-processors, but does not require controller authorization.
results.condition_check: The regulation's trigger is engaging a sub-processor without prior written authorization; the contract has no corresponding authorization condition.
results.modality_check: The regulation imposes a prohibition absent authorization; the contract imposes different affirmative duties and does not replicate the authorization requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract adds written agreements and monitoring/reporting obligations, which are different in subject and do not substitute for prior controller authorization.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall conclude a written sub-processor agreement with any Sub-Processors. Such an agreement shall at minimum provide the same data protection obligations as the ones applicable to the Data Processor, including the obligations under this Data Processor Agreement. The Data Processor shall on an ongoing basis monitor and control its Sub- Processors’ compliance with the Applicable Law. Documentation of such monitoring and control shall be provided to the Data Controller if so requested in writing.

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires the processor to remain fully liable to the controller for a sub-processor's obligations, but the contract clause instead addresses remuneration for changes to instructions and exemption from liability for non-performance of the main agreement; it does not allocate liability for sub-processor obligations.
results.condition_check: No relevant condition overlap; the regulation is an ongoing liability rule, while the contract concerns changed instructions and impossibility of performance.
results.modality_check: The contract uses entitlement/exemption language, not an obligation to remain liable.
results.other_constraints_check: Different object and target: sub-processor obligations versus changed instructions/main agreement performance.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor is also entitled to remuneration for any time and material used to adapt and change the processing activities in order to comply with any changes to the Data Controller’s Instruction, including implementation costs and additional costs required to deliver the Main Services due to the change in the Instruction. The Data Processor is exempted from liability for non-performance with the Main Agreement if the performance of the obligations under the Main Agreement would be in conflict with any changed Instruction or if contractual delivery in accordance with the changed Instruction is impossible. results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to contractually impose the Article 28(3) GDPR obligations on engaged sub-processors. The contract clause instead concerns general authorization to engage sub-processors and prior written notice/objection rights for the controller; it does not state that the same Article 28(3) obligations are passed down to sub-processors.
results.condition_check: The regulation is triggered when the processor engages sub-processors. The contract clause is triggered by the processor wishing to engage third parties and by controller notification/objection, which is a different and narrower/different condition set.
results.modality_check: The regulation imposes a mandatory duty ('shall impose'). The contract clause grants authorization and sets notice/objection mechanics, but does not impose the required downstream contractual obligation on sub-processors.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation requires doing this 'by way of contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law' and specifically with the same Article 28(3) obligations. The contract clause instead addresses timing of notice before agreements and processing, plus controller objection timing, which are unrelated constraints to the required downstream contractual imposition.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor is given general authorization to engage third-parties to process the Personal Data (“Sub-Processors”) without obtaining any further written, specific authorization from the Data Controller, provided that the Data Processor notifies the Data Controller in writing about the identity of a potential Sub-Processor (and its processors, if any) before any agreements are made with the relevant Sub-Processors and before the relevant Sub-Processor processes any of the Personal Data. If the Data Controller wish to object to the relevant Sub- Processor, the Data Controller shall give notice hereof in writing within seven

results.condition_check: No substantive condition is stated in the contract text.
results.modality_check: No obligation modality is stated in the contract text.
results.other_constraints_check: No timing, manner, target, or other operative constraints are stated in the contract text.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: the regulation requires the DPA to state the duration of processing, and the contract specifies a processing period of three months after termination, which is the duration of processing.
results.condition_check: Compatible: the contract ties the post-termination processing period to necessity and applicable law, but still states the duration.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the contract uses mandatory language for the processing period and does not weaken the requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible: the timing is specific and at least as precise as the regulation’s duration requirement.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. The Data Processor’s processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data in the three months after the termination of this Data Processor Agreement shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires the DPA to contain the duration of processing, and the contract states that processing continues for up to three months after termination, which directly specifies the processing duration.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The contract adds a qualifier that continuation is only to the extent necessary and required under applicable law, which is not weaker than the regulation.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract uses mandatory language for the processing period and thus provides the required content with sufficient force.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The timing/duration is stated explicitly as three months after termination, which satisfies the requirement to contain the duration of processing.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. results.core_alignment_check: Same general security domain, but the contract only requires immediate breach notification; it does not impose the processor's duty to assist the controller in ensuring processing security.
results.condition_check: Both concern security-related incidents, but the regulation is a general assistance duty for ensuring security of processing, while the contract is triggered only by a personal data breach.
results.modality_check: The contract uses 'shall', but it is for a different obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract adds immediate notice and a defined breach condition; these are not the same as assisting with security measures.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall give immediate notice to the Data Controller if a breach of the data security occurs, that can lead to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure of or access to, personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed re the Personal Data processed on behalf of the Data Controller (a “Personal Data Breach”).

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. Both provisions address the processor engaging sub-processors and the controller's written authorization. The contract gives general authorization subject to prior written notice and an objection window, which is the same real-world mechanism as prior specific or general written authorization.
results.condition_check: Compatible. The regulation requires prior specific or general written authorization; the contract provides general authorization with prior written notice and an opportunity for the controller to object before processing begins.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The contract is at least as strict because it does not permit engagement without prior controller involvement; it conditions engagement on notice and preserves controller objection rights, which meets the authorization requirement.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The timing is prior to agreements and prior to processing, which is equivalent to or stricter than prior authorization. The written form requirement is also preserved.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor is given general authorization to engage third-parties to process the Personal Data (“Sub-Processors”) without obtaining any further written, specific authorization from the Data Controller, provided that the Data Processor notifies the Data Controller in writing about the identity of a potential Sub-Processor (and its processors, if any) before any agreements are made with the relevant Sub-Processors and before the relevant Sub-Processor processes any of the Personal Data. If the Data Controller wish to object to the relevant Sub- Processor, the Data Controller shall give notice hereof in writing within seven

results.core_alignment_check: Yes: both concern the processor's responsibility for sub-processors' obligations, but the contract focuses on imposing equivalent obligations on sub-processors and monitoring them, not on the processor remaining fully liable to the controller.
results.condition_check: Compatible: no special triggering condition in the regulation beyond sub-processor performance; the contract addresses sub-processors generally.
results.modality_check: Weaker/misaligned: the regulation requires full liability of the processor to the controller; the contract requires written sub-processor agreements and monitoring, which are different and do not expressly preserve full liability.
results.other_constraints_check: No conflicting timing/location constraints; however the contract omits the key liability-to-controller commitment and instead adds implementation measures.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall conclude a written sub-processor agreement with any Sub-Processors. Such an agreement shall at minimum provide the same data protection obligations as the ones applicable to the Data Processor, including the obligations under this Data Processor Agreement. The Data Processor shall on an ongoing basis monitor and control its Sub- Processors’ compliance with the Applicable Law. Documentation of such monitoring and control shall be provided to the Data Controller if so requested in writing.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the DPA to state the duration of the processing, i.e. a content term about processing duration. The contract instead requires the processor to have and maintain a register of processing activities; that is a different record-keeping duty and does not itself state the duration of processing.
results.condition_check: No relevant condition in either clause.
results.modality_check: The contract uses 'shall', but modality is irrelevant because the object differs.
results.other_constraints_check: No matching timing, manner, target, or location constraint; the register obligation is a different duty.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall have and maintain a register of processing activities in accordance with GDPR, article 32 (2).

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires the processor to make available information necessary to demonstrate compliance with Article 28 obligations to the controller, but the sub-item only states a general compliance-with-law commitment and does not itself provide or make available the required information. The parent clause is a pointer to documentation substantiating something, but the sub-item content does not specify the information duty.
results.condition_check: Compatible: the regulation is triggered by the need to demonstrate compliance; the parent clause adds a written request condition, which is a reasonable and narrower trigger for providing documentation.
results.modality_check: Compatible: the parent clause uses 'shall' and imposes an obligation, which is at least as strong as the regulation's duty.
results.other_constraints_check: Not fully aligned: the regulation requires making information available to the controller; the contract instead speaks of providing documentation substantiating compliance after written request, which is narrower and does not clearly cover all necessary information.
results.contract_source_text: the Data Processor complies with the Applicable Law in respect of the processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data.

results.core_alignment_check: The contract text is only a heading/topic label ('Data protection impact assessments and prior consultation') and does not itself state any DPIA content items such as the processing description, necessity/proportionality assessment, risk assessment, or measures to address risks.

results.modality_check: No operative modality stated; only a heading.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to notify the controller after becoming aware of a personal data breach. The contract parent clause requires the processor to maintain a register of personal data breaches, and the sub-item specifies the description/content of that register entry. This is breach-record content, not a notification to the controller, so the core obligation is different.
results.condition_check: The regulation is triggered after becoming aware of a breach; the contract sub-item is framed as register content and does not impose the same trigger or notification condition.
results.modality_check: The regulation imposes a mandatory notification duty. The contract imposes a mandatory register duty, but not the notification duty itself.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation requires notification without undue delay to the controller. The contract instead concerns the nature and statistics of the breach entry in a register, which is a different target and purpose.
results.contract_source_text: A description of the nature of the Personal Data Breach, including, if possible, the categories and the approximate number of affected Data Subjects and the categories and the approximate number of affected registrations of personal data.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires that a personal data breach notification include specified information (nature of breach, DPO contact details, consequences, mitigation measures). The contract only requires the processor to maintain a register of breaches and says the register shall include unspecified items, but it does not itself state the required notification contents or that they are included.

results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory language for maintaining a register, but it does not impose the notification-content obligation required by the regulation.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation is about the contents of a breach notification; the contract is about keeping a register, which is a different duty and does not supply the required content.

results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation permits processing without documented instructions when required by Union or Member State law; the contract instead imposes confidentiality and a general prohibition on processing contrary to instructions, with only controller-written consent as an exception. results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. In the same period, the Data Processor is entitled to include the Personal Data in the Data Processor’s backup. The Data Processor’s processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data in the three months after the termination of this Data Processor Agreement shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction.

shared_groups: Data Return Deletion And Post-Termination Handling
results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor, after services end, to return or delete all personal data to the controller. The contract instead allows continued processing for up to three months after termination if necessary under applicable law and permits backup inclusion; it does not require return or deletion to the controller.
results.condition_check: The regulation is triggered after the end of the provision of services relating to processing; the contract is triggered after termination and adds a legal-necessity qualifier, but the substantive duty is different.
results.modality_check: The regulation imposes a mandatory return-or-delete obligation. The contract gives permission/entitlement to continue processing and backup inclusion, which is not an equal-or-greater obligation to return or delete.
results.other_constraints_check: The timing is not equivalent: the contract authorizes processing for three months after termination rather than requiring return or deletion at the end of services. The target/object also differs because it concerns continued processing and backups, not return/delete to the controller.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. In the same period, the Data Processor is entitled to include the Personal Data in the Data Processor’s backup. The Data Processor’s processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data in the three months after the termination of this Data Processor Agreement shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation sets a processor’s liability rule for damage caused by processing, limited to two specific fault conditions (non-compliance with GDPR processor obligations or acting outside/contrary to controller instructions). The contract instead imposes a general cap on each party’s cumulated liability for breach of contract based on prior payments. Same broad subject of liability, but not the same obligation.
results.condition_check: Not aligned. The regulation’s trigger is liability for damage caused by processing only in specified misconduct situations; the contract’s trigger is any circumstances leading to a breach of contract, with no reference to GDPR processor obligations or unlawful controller instructions.
results.modality_check: The contract is mandatory as a liability cap, but it is not the same kind of obligation and does not preserve the regulation’s liability rule.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract adds a monetary cap and a 12-month lookback/proportional calculation, which are different constraints from the regulation’s substantive liability conditions and do not address them.
results.contract_source_text: Each party’s cumulated liability under this Data Processor Agreement is limited to the payments made under the Main Agreement in the 12 months before the occurrence of the circumstances leading to a breach of contract. If the Data Processor Agreement has not been in force for 12 months before the occurrence of the circumstances leading to a breach of contract, the limited liability amount shall be calculated proportionately based on the actual performed payments.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to remain liable to the controller for sub-processor obligations; the contract instead requires the controller to indemnify the processor for additional costs from legal changes. Different actor, duty, object, and recipient.
results.condition_check: Not applicable / different trigger: the regulation is unconditional liability for sub-processor performance, while the contract is conditional indemnification for cost increases due to changes in law.
results.modality_check: Different modality and duty: the contract imposes an indemnity obligation, not liability for sub-processor obligations.
results.other_constraints_check: No matching target, scope, or subject matter; the clause concerns costs from legal changes, not sub-processor performance or liability to the controller.
results.contract_source_text: If changes to the Applicable Law, including new guidance or courts practice, result in additional costs to the Data Processor, the Data Controller shall indemnify the Data Processor of such documented costs. 8. Breach and liability

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires that, under general written authorization, the controller be informed in advance of intended sub-processor changes and have the right to object to additions or replacements of sub-processors. The parent clause gives the processor general authorization, requires prior written notice of the identity of a potential sub-processor before agreements are made and before processing starts, and grants the controller a right to object within seven calendar days. The sub-item stating that absence of objections is deemed consent is consistent with that objection-right framework.
results.condition_check: Compatible. Both apply in the setting of general written authorization and prior notice of intended sub-processor changes/additions/replacements.
results.modality_check: Compatible. The regulation requires a right to object; the contract expressly gives the controller the right to object within a defined period, which is at least as strong.
results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The contract is stricter on timing by requiring notice before agreements are made and before processing begins, and it sets a seven-day objection window after notification, which does not weaken the right to object.
results.contract_source_text: calendar days from receiving the notification from the Data Processor. Absence of any objections from the Data Controller shall be deemed a consent to the relevant Sub-Processor.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation is about the processor's liability for damage caused by processing, limited to cases of processor GDPR non-compliance or acting outside/contrary to controller instructions. The contract clause is about the controller indemnifying the processor for additional costs caused by changes in law/guidance/court practice. Different actor, different object, different trigger, different remedy.
results.condition_check: Not compatible: regulation triggers on damage caused by processing plus processor non-compliance or acting outside instructions; contract triggers on changes in applicable law causing additional costs.
results.modality_check: Not comparable as a liability rule; the contract imposes an indemnity obligation on the controller, not a processor-liability limitation or allocation matching the regulation.
results.other_constraints_check: Different subject matter and scope; no overlap on damage caused by processing, GDPR processor obligations, or lawful instructions.

The Data Processor shall on an ongoing basis monitor and control its Sub- Processors’ compliance with the Applicable Law. Documentation of such monitoring and control shall be provided to the Data Controller if so requested in writing.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to make available information necessary to demonstrate compliance with Article 28 obligations to the controller. results.core_alignment_check: Yes. Both address the processor's use/engagement of sub-processors and the controller's authorization/approval for that use.

results.other_constraints_check: Compatible. The timing is prior/at inception, which satisfies the prior authorization condition; no weaker manner, target, location, or cause constraints appear.

results.condition_check: No relevant condition overlap; the clause's three-month post-termination period and legal-necessity qualifier are unrelated to the requirement to contain the nature and purpose of processing.
results.modality_check: The clause uses mandatory and permissive language for post-termination processing, but modality is irrelevant because it does not address the required content.
results.other_constraints_check: No matching content on the required subject; timing/location constraints about post-termination processing do not satisfy the requirement to set out nature and purpose.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. In the same period, the Data Processor is entitled to include the Personal Data in the Data Processor’s backup. The Data Processor’s processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data in the three months after the termination of this Data Processor Agreement shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to remain fully liable to the controller for sub-processor performance. The contract instead requires written sub-processor agreements with equivalent data protection obligations and ongoing monitoring/control of sub-processors, plus providing documentation on request. This addresses oversight and flow-down obligations, not the processor's continuing liability to the controller.
results.condition_check: No special triggering condition in the regulation beyond sub-processor performance; the contract's request-based documentation duty is a different condition and does not supply liability.
results.modality_check: The regulation imposes a liability obligation. The contract imposes contracting/monitoring/documentation duties, but not an express full-liability undertaking.
results.other_constraints_check: The target and object differ: liability for performance versus contractual flow-down and monitoring of compliance. The contract is substantively adjacent but not the same duty.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall conclude a written sub-processor agreement with any Sub-Processors. results.core_alignment_check: No: the regulation permits the processor to process personal data without documented instructions when required by Union or Member State law; the contract text merely identifies applicable data protection legislation and does not state any permission/exception to process without instructions.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to make available information necessary to demonstrate compliance with Article 28 obligations to the controller. results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to assist the controller in responding to data subject rights requests; the contract instead requires providing a personal data breaches register to the controller or data protection agency on written request. results.modality_check: Both are mandatory when triggered, but they concern different obligations.
results.other_constraints_check: Different target and purpose; the contract is about breach-register disclosure, not assistance with rights requests.
results.contract_source_text: The register of Personal Data Breaches shall be provided to the Data Controller in copy if so requested in writing by the Data Controller or the relevant Data Protection Agency.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the breach notification itself to include specific content: nature of the personal data breach, DPO contact details, consequences, and mitigation measures. The contract clause only requires the processor to give immediate notice to the controller if a personal data breach occurs; it defines the breach but does not require inclusion of the listed notification contents.
results.condition_check: Compatible: both are triggered by a personal data breach.
results.modality_check: The contract is mandatory and at least as strong on timing ('immediate notice').
results.other_constraints_check: Timing is stricter, but the required informational content is missing; the clause is about notice of occurrence, not the required contents of the notification.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall give immediate notice to the Data Controller if a breach of the data security occurs, that can lead to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure of or access to, personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed re the Personal Data processed on behalf of the Data Controller (a “Personal Data Breach”).

results.core_alignment_check: Gap: the regulation requires the DPA to contain the categories of data subjects, but the contract only states processing is limited to the controller's documented instructions and to delivering the main services; it does not name or set out any categories of data subjects.
results.condition_check: No relevant condition in the regulation beyond inclusion in the DPA; the contract does not provide the required content.
results.modality_check: The contract's 'may only' is restrictive, but it is about processing limits, not about stating categories of data subjects.
results.other_constraints_check: No matching content on categories of data subjects is present; the purpose limitation and reference to the main agreement are different constraints.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor may only act and process the Personal Data in accordance with the documented instruction from the Data Controller (the ”Instruction”). The Instruction at the time of entering into this Data Processor Agreement is that the Data Processor may only process the Personal Data with the purpose of delivering the Main Services as described in the Main Agreement.

results.modality_check: The contract uses mandatory language, but it is for a different duty.
results.other_constraints_check: The timing and recipient constraints do not match the security-assistance obligation.
results.contract_source_text: If the Data Processor receives a request from a data subject for the exercise of the data subject’s rights under the Applicable Law and such request is related to the Personal Data of the Data Controller, the Data Processor must immediately forward the request to the Data Controller and must refrain from responding to the person directly.

results.core_alignment_check: Yes. The regulation requires the DPA to state the nature and purpose of the processing. results.other_constraints_check: No substantive overlap on scope, timing, or target; the regulation is about liability for processing damage under specific GDPR circumstances, while the contract is about internal contract breach consequences.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires implementing/maintaining security measures for processing (e.g., pseudonymisation, encryption, confidentiality, integrity, availability, resilience, recovery, testing/evaluation). results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to take measures to ensure security of processing under Article 32. The contract requires the processor to treat all personal data as strictly confidential and prohibits copying, transferring, or otherwise processing it contrary to instructions unless the controller agrees in writing. results.modality_check: The regulation imposes a mandatory duty. The contract uses mandatory 'shall' and a prohibition ('may not'), which is at least as strong.
results.other_constraints_check: The regulation is about security measures required under Article 32. The contract focuses on confidentiality and instruction compliance, which is narrower than the full Article 32 security obligation and omits broader security measures such as technical and organizational safeguards, resilience, testing, and incident handling.

results.condition_check: Compatible: the contract’s restriction applies unless the controller has agreed in writing; this is not weaker than the security obligation.
results.modality_check: Mandatory/restrictive: 'shall' and 'may not' are at least as strong as the regulation’s 'shall'.
results.other_constraints_check: Substantively compatible and at least as strict on manner/target of processing; confidentiality and instruction-compliance are narrower but within the security/protection domain, not contradictory.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall treat all the Personal Data as strictly confidential information. The Personal Data may not be copied, transferred or otherwise processed in conflict with the Instruction, unless the Data Controller in writing has agreed hereto.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the DPA to state the types of personal data processed, while the contract clause only says the agreement remains in force until termination.
results.condition_check: Not applicable; the clause concerns duration of the agreement, not the required data-type content.
results.modality_check: Not applicable; there is no obligation to contain the types of personal data.
results.other_constraints_check: Not applicable; the clause addresses term/termination, not the required content.
results.contract_source_text: The Data processor Agreement shall remain in force until the Main Agreement is terminated. 10. Termination

shared_groups: Assistance With Data Subject And Regulatory Rights
results.core_alignment_check: processor assistance with DPIA compliance vs providing a personal data breaches register on request; different obligation and different subject matter
results.condition_check: Regulation concerns assisting with DPIA compliance; contract concerns disclosure of a breach register upon written request by controller or data protection agency
results.modality_check: Regulation imposes a mandatory assistance duty; contract imposes a conditional disclosure duty, but on a different topic
results.other_constraints_check: No substantive overlap in object, recipient, or purpose; the contract clause is about breach records, not DPIA assistance
results.contract_source_text: The register of Personal Data Breaches shall be provided to the Data Controller in copy if so requested in writing by the Data Controller or the relevant Data Protection Agency.

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to assist the controller in ensuring security of processing; the contract instead imposes a direct security/access-restriction duty on the processor for its own processing obligations, not assistance to the controller.
results.condition_check: Compatible in subject matter (security/access control), but the triggering situation differs: the regulation is an assistance duty to the controller, while the contract is an independent operational restriction.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a mandatory 'shall', which is not weaker than the regulation's obligation, but it is directed to a different obligation.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract's constraint on access being limited to necessary/relevant employees is a security measure, but it does not include the required recipient/object of assisting the controller.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall ensure that access to the Personal Data is restricted to only the employees to whom it is necessary and relevant to process the Personal Data in order for the Data Processor to perform its obligations under the Main Agreement and this Data Processor Agreement.

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the DPA to contain the nature and purpose of the processing. The contract text only says the agreement regulates the processor’s processing of personal data on behalf of the controller and is attached as appendix A; it does not itself state the nature and purpose of the processing.
results.condition_check: No special condition beyond the DPA containing the information; the clause does not supply that information.
results.modality_check: The regulation is mandatory, but the contract clause is only a general introductory description and attachment reference, not an obligation to include the required content.
results.other_constraints_check: The clause points to the agreement’s subject matter and attachment status, but does not set out the nature or purpose in-line or elsewhere in the provided text.
results.contract_source_text: This agreement re processing of personal data (the ”Data Processor Agreement”) regulates Processor, Company registration no. 25163258 (the ”Data Processor”) processing of personal data on behalf of the customer (the ”Data Controller”) and is attached as appendix A to the Processor subscription agreement (the ”Main Agreement”), in which the parties have agreed the terms for the Data Processor’s delivery of services to the Data Controller (the ”Main Services”). 2. Legislation

results.core_alignment_check: No. The regulation requires the processor to implement appropriate technical and organisational security measures to ensure risk-appropriate security. The contract clause instead imposes a breach-notification duty to the controller if a personal data security breach occurs.
results.condition_check: The triggering condition differs: the regulation is an ongoing security-measures obligation based on risk, while the contract is triggered only after a data security breach that can lead to certain harms.
results.modality_check: The contract uses a mandatory notice obligation, but it is a different duty from the regulation's mandatory implementation of security measures.
results.other_constraints_check: The contract's timing ('immediate notice') and object (notification of breaches) do not match the regulation's manner/target of implementing technical and organisational measures for security.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall give immediate notice to the Data Controller if a breach of the data security occurs, that can lead to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure of or access to, personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed re the Personal Data processed on behalf of the Data Controller (a “Personal Data Breach”).

results.core_alignment_check: The regulation requires the processor to process personal data only on documented instructions from the controller. The contract clause addresses adapting/changing processing activities to comply with changes to the controller's instructions and excuses non-performance if performance conflicts with changed instructions, but it does not itself state that processing must be carried out on documented instructions or that the processor must follow documented instructions as the governing basis for processing.
results.condition_check: The regulation's condition is that instructions be documented; the contract refers to changed instructions but does not require documentation as the condition for processing.
results.modality_check: The regulation imposes a mandatory processing obligation. The contract mainly grants entitlement to remuneration and exempts liability; it does not impose the same mandatory instruction-based processing duty.
results.other_constraints_check: The subject matter overlaps with instruction changes, but the contract is about remuneration and liability for adapting services, not the substantive rule that processing occurs only on documented controller instructions.
results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor is also entitled to remuneration for any time and material used to adapt and change the processing activities in order to comply with any changes to the Data Controller’s Instruction, including implementation costs and additional costs required to deliver the Main Services due to the change in the Instruction. The Data Processor is exempted from liability for non-performance with the Main Agreement if the performance of the obligations under the Main Agreement would be in conflict with any changed Instruction or if contractual delivery in accordance with the changed Instruction is impossible. results.contract_source_text: The Data Processor shall continue to process the Personal Data for up to three months after the termination of the Data Processor Agreement to the extent it is necessary and required under the Applicable Law. In the same period, the Data Processor is entitled to include the Personal Data in the Data Processor’s backup. The Data Processor’s processing of the Data Controller’s Personal Data in the three months after the termination of this Data Processor Agreement shall be considered as being in accordance with the Instruction.
